The URET (Universal Robustness Evaluation Toolkit) is a proposed framework that aims to generate adversarial inputs for various machine learning tasks and input types, beyond just image domain tasks. The current evasion attacks, designed primarily for image classification tasks, have limitations when applied to other input types due to the non-differentiable nature of feature extraction and the lack of continuous, numerical, and independent features.

The text discusses a research on a general-purpose evasion attack framework for AI systems, named URET. The framework generates adversarial inputs as a graph exploration problem, bypassing the issue of non-differentiable feature extraction. It defines a set of functionality and semantic preserving transformations for various common data representations, allowing users to define their own transformations for given data.

The URET (Our) proposed attack, as outlined in the text, computes the Jacobian Saliency map of a sample and identifies the feature indices that are likely to cause adversarial misclassification in machine learning models. The attack modifies 0-valued features, adding new capabilities without removing any existing ones. This approach is adaptable to various binary feature representations and has been leveraged by other works.

The limitation of this type of attack is the requirement for interpretable, independent feature representations to easily map modifications back to the original input representation. Other works

Counterfit is a general adversarial attack and evaluation framework that simplifies the process for security experts by abstracting away details such as hyperparameter tuning and attack selection. It leverages three existing libraries - Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART), TextAttack, and AugLy - to support a wider range of input types and task domains compared to traditional adversarial attacks.

The text discusses a framework for finding adversarial inputs in graphs, such as websites or networks. The framework uses a graph explorer to traverse vertices (nodes) and edges (connections) in the graph. The exploration aims to find a vertex that is within a certain number of hops from the original vertex and is misclassified by a given classifier.

The text discusses a research on an AI threat model that uses machine learning to generate adversarial inputs for various AI systems. The main focus is on two algorithms: Lookup Table and Model Guided.

The Lookup Table algorithm generates a table of estimated edge weights for every unique edge encountered during training, which is used to rank edges during exploration. However, it doesn't consider the current vertex state, resulting in a lower success rate compared to the Brute-Force approach. The text discusses research on creating adversarial examples for domain name generation algorithms (DGA) used by malware to avoid blacklisting. The researchers used a DGA classifier and a variety of exploration algorithms to generate adversarial domain names that could fool the classifier.

The attack mechanism involves transforming a domain name to create an adversarial example by adding, deleting, or substituting alphanumeric characters. The transformed domain names are then classified by the DGA classifier, and if misclassified, they are considered adversarial examples.

The text discusses AI threat research focusing on creating adversarial examples for malware classification, specifically for binary files. The research involves using a framework to generate adversarial objects that can bypass malware classifiers.

The attack mechanism involves using Beam Search with a Model Guided ranking algorithm to perturb binary files in a way that maintains their semantic correctness and functionality. The perturbations are guided by the L2 distance between the current perturbation vector and a target perturbation vector generated using the Hop-SkipJump algorithm.

The text discusses a research on an adversarial attack framework designed to generate adversarial inputs for AI systems, regardless of the modality or task. The attack mechanism involves transforming malware binaries to evade detection by AI systems. The text discusses an AI threat research focused on generating adversarial attacks for Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA). The attack mechanism involves a framework that iteratively selects actions, updates the neural policy, and repeats the process for all inputs in the training set. The framework was tested on numerical data, specifically modifying 13 out of 20 numerical features with a limit of modifying no more than 30% of the feature's current value.

The results showed an increase in success rate and a decrease in average time per sample compared to previous experiments
